cards.back: Service port (out-of-band mgmt). Management interface (in-band mgmt, AP-WLC CAPWAP). Virtual interface (1.1.1.1 — used for guest portal redirects, mobility). AP-Manager (older models — terminates CAPWAP; merged into mgmt on newer). Dynamic interfaces (per WLAN/VLAN client traffic).

cards.front: Switch-port config for an AP that uses CAPWAP back to a WLC?
cards.back: Access port on the AP's management VLAN: `switchport mode access`, `switchport access vlan X`. PoE for power: `power inline auto`. The AP gets DHCP, learns WLC IP via DHCP option 43 or DNS (CISCO-CAPWAP-CONTROLLER), forms CAPWAP tunnel.

cards.front: AP discovers its WLC. What 4 mechanisms can it use?
cards.back: (1) DHCP option 43 (vendor-specific, returns WLC IP). (2) DNS lookup of CISCO-CAPWAP-CONTROLLER.<domain>. (3) Layer-2 broadcast on the AP's local subnet. (4) Statically configured controller IP via prior console/GUI provisioning. The AP probes all known WLCs, picks the primary or least-loaded, and joins.

cards.front: Configure a basic WLAN on a Cisco WLC (high-level steps via GUI).
cards.back: (1) Create a Dynamic Interface for the user VLAN. (2) WLANs → Create New → enter SSID, profile name. (3) General tab: enable Status, map to interface. (4) Security tab: choose WPA2/WPA3 + 802.1X or PSK. (5) QoS, advanced → save. (6) Apply.

cards.front: What is RRM and what does it do?
cards.back: Radio Resource Management — WLC feature that automatically tunes AP radios: channel selection (DCA — Dynamic Channel Assignment) and transmit power (TPC — Transmit Power Control). Adjusts dynamically to interference and AP density. Reduces manual RF planning.

cards.front: Why use a separate VLAN/SSID for guest Wi-Fi?
cards.back: Isolation — guests can't reach internal resources. Often combined with: captive portal for sign-in, guest-anchor (tunneling guest traffic to a DMZ WLC), strict ACLs, DHCP lease limits, and bandwidth caps. Keeps guest devices off the corporate network entirely.

cards.front: WLC port types: Service, Distribution System, Console, Redundancy — what is each?
cards.back: Service port: out-of-band management; connects to a switch ACCESS port (separate VLAN from data). Distribution system ports: production traffic — carry CAPWAP, client data; usually a LAG/trunk to the switch fabric. Console port: serial CLI access. Redundancy port: direct connection to the standby WLC for HA pair (state sync). All ports are physical.

cards.front: WLC interface types: Management, Redundancy-Management, Virtual, Service-Port, Dynamic — what is each?
cards.back: Management: in-band mgmt; CAPWAP tunnels terminate here. Redundancy-Management: manages standby WLC in HA pair. Virtual: uses a private IP (e.g., 1.1.1.1) for client-facing services like DHCP relay, web auth redirects, mobility. Service-Port: bound to the physical service port. Dynamic: maps WLANs to client VLANs (one dynamic interface per client VLAN). All interfaces are LOGICAL.

cards.front: WLAN QoS profiles on Cisco WLC: Platinum, Gold, Silver, Bronze — what traffic class does each represent?
cards.back: Platinum = Voice (highest priority). Gold = Video. Silver = Best Effort (DEFAULT for new WLANs). Bronze = Background (lowest). Maps to 802.11e WMM access categories.

cards.front: Does a WLC support LACP/PAgP for link aggregation?
cards.back: No — Cisco WLCs only support STATIC LAG (mode `on` / unconditional). LACP and PAgP are not supported. The connecting switch must also be configured for static EtherChannel (`channel-group N mode on`).
